title: EEG Signal Generator
description: Generative AI models (GAN, VAE, Diffusion) for synthesizing realistic EEG brain signals. Trained on neuroscience datasets with ARM-optimized ONNX inference on Raspberry Pi.
long_description:
This project trains three classes of generative models — a Wasserstein GAN with gradient penalty, a convolutional Variational Autoencoder, and a DDPM-style Diffusion model — to synthesize realistic multi-channel EEG time-series. Each architecture was evaluated against real recordings using spectral coherence, power spectral density overlap, and a trained discriminator score to confirm statistical indistinguishability from genuine brain activity.

The training pipeline uses PyTorch with a custom EEG dataset loader that applies bandpass filtering (0.5–45 Hz), z-score normalization per channel, and epoch extraction at 256 Hz. The GAN generator conditions on frequency-band labels (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma) to produce class-specific signals, enabling targeted data augmentation for downstream classification tasks.

All three models were exported to ONNX with INT8 post-training quantization and benchmarked on a Raspberry Pi 4. The VAE achieves 12ms per sample — fast enough for real-time applications. The interactive demo lets you select a model type and frequency band, then visualize the generated signal alongside its FFT spectrum.

title: Medical Research Chatbot
description: RAG-powered chatbot that retrieves and summarizes medical research from PubMed. Uses embedding search and LLM generation with quantized models.
long_description:
This Retrieval-Augmented Generation system ingests PubMed abstracts via the NCBI E-utilities API and indexes them in a FAISS vector store using a sentence-transformer embedding model (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, 22M parameters). At query time, the top-k most relevant abstracts are retrieved and injected into the context window of a quantized LLM for grounded answer generation, with citations linking back to the source PubMed IDs.

The retrieval pipeline uses chunked indexing with 512-token windows and 64-token overlap to preserve sentence boundaries. A hybrid BM25 + dense retrieval strategy is used for reranking, improving recall on rare medical terminology that embedding models may not capture well. The LLM (Phi-3-mini, INT4 GGUF) is served via llama.cpp to keep memory usage below 1 GB on the Raspberry Pi.

The Streamlit interface supports multi-turn conversation with a summary panel showing which source abstracts were used in each response. Query latency averages 4–8 seconds end-to-end on the Pi, covering embedding inference, FAISS search, and LLM generation.

title: Handwritten Text Recognition
description: CNN + BiLSTM handwritten text line recognizer trained on the IAM Handwriting Dataset. Uses CTC decoding with per-character confidence scores. Exported to ONNX with INT8 quantization for efficient inference on Raspberry Pi.
long_description:
This handwritten text recognition system uses a CNN + BiLSTM architecture trained on the IAM Handwriting Dataset. The convolutional backbone (4 blocks: 1->64->128->256->512 channels) extracts visual features, which are then processed by a 2-layer bidirectional LSTM for sequence modeling. CTC (Connectionist Temporal Classification) loss enables training without character-level alignment.

The model recognizes pre-segmented text lines with a vocabulary of 82 characters. It was exported to ONNX format with INT8 quantization (46 MB -> 11.6 MB) for efficient inference on the Raspberry Pi's ARM processor. Per-character confidence scores are extracted during greedy CTC decoding.

The interactive demo supports image upload, freehand drawing, and pre-loaded sample images. A preprocessing pipeline visualization shows each transformation step: grayscale conversion, height normalization to 128px, and width padding to 2048px.

title: Bomberman RL Agent
description: Reinforcement learning agent trained to play Bomberman. Watch the agent navigate mazes, place bombs strategically, and avoid explosions in real-time.
long_description:
This project trains a Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent with prioritized experience replay to play a custom Bomberman environment built on top of a grid-world simulation. The state representation encodes board layout, bomb timers, explosion radii, and agent positions as a multi-channel tensor fed into a convolutional policy network.

The reward shaping is a key challenge: sparse win/loss rewards alone cause slow convergence, so a dense auxiliary reward was designed around crate destruction, safe escape routing after bomb placement, and opponent proximity penalties. Curriculum learning progressively increases maze complexity and the number of opponents across training phases.

The trained agent was exported to ONNX for deployment and achieves sub-5ms inference per step, enabling the real-time demo. The Streamlit interface renders the game board frame-by-frame, annotating the agent's Q-value estimates for each action and highlighting the planned escape path after each bomb placement.

title: This Portfolio Website
description: Full-stack portfolio hosted on a Raspberry Pi 4. React frontend, FastAPI backend, Nginx reverse proxy, Cloudflare tunnel — all running on a $55 ARM computer.
long_description:
The entire portfolio runs on a Raspberry Pi 4 (4 GB RAM, aarch64) in a home lab, exposed to the public internet via a Cloudflare tunnel — no port forwarding or static IP required. Nginx sits in front and routes traffic: static React assets are served directly from disk, API requests are proxied to a FastAPI process managed by systemd.

The backend uses async SQLAlchemy with aiosqlite for a zero-config SQLite database, and the FastAPI app follows a routers/models/schemas layering pattern. The React frontend is built with Vite and TypeScript, styled entirely with Tailwind CSS utility classes, and communicates with the backend via a `/api` prefix that Nginx strips before forwarding.

title: Neural Data Analysis Toolkit
description: Python toolkit for processing and analyzing neural recording data. Includes spike sorting, spectral analysis, and visualization utilities for neuroscience research.
long_description:
A Python library for end-to-end processing of extracellular neural recordings. The pipeline covers raw signal preprocessing (common-average referencing, bandpass filtering via SciPy), threshold-based spike detection, PCA-based feature extraction, and k-means / GMM clustering for unit assignment. Output is compatible with the Neurodata Without Borders (NWB) format for interoperability with downstream analysis tools.

Spectral analysis utilities include Welch PSD estimation, multi-taper coherence between electrode channels, and time-frequency spectrograms using the short-time Fourier transform. Matplotlib-based visualization functions produce publication-ready figures for spike rasters, peri-stimulus time histograms, and inter-spike interval distributions.

title: ML Model Optimizer for ARM
description: Pipeline for quantizing and optimizing ML models for ARM deployment. Supports ONNX and TFLite with INT8 quantization and benchmarking on Raspberry Pi.
long_description:
A CLI-driven optimization pipeline that takes a trained PyTorch or TensorFlow model and produces a deployment-ready artifact for ARM hardware. For ONNX targets, it runs ONNX Runtime's quantization toolkit with a representative calibration dataset to compute per-layer INT8 scales. For TFLite targets, it applies TensorFlow Lite's post-training integer quantization with optional per-channel scaling.

A built-in benchmarking harness measures latency (p50/p95), peak RSS memory, and accuracy degradation relative to the FP32 baseline. Results are written to a JSON report and optionally plotted with matplotlib. The tool was used to produce all the ONNX models deployed in this portfolio, reducing model sizes by 60–75% with less than 1% accuracy drop.

title: Research Paper Tracker
description: CLI tool for tracking, annotating, and organizing research papers. Integrates with arXiv and PubMed APIs for automatic metadata retrieval.
long_description:
A command-line tool built with Typer and SQLite (via SQLAlchemy) for managing a personal reading list of research papers. Given an arXiv ID or PubMed PMID, it automatically fetches the title, authors, abstract, publication date, and DOI using the respective public APIs. Papers are stored locally and can be tagged, annotated with personal notes, and marked with reading status (unread, in-progress, done).

Search and filter commands allow querying by tag, author, keyword in title or abstract, or date range. Bibtex export is supported for generating reference lists. The tool also supports a `digest` command that prints a weekly summary of newly added papers, making it easy to stay on top of a reading queue.
